items.title: Digest #164: GitHub Actions Pain, Wiz Joins Google, Handling Billions of AWS Lambda Invocations, CDK Migration and Prometheus
items.author: Mohamed Labouardy
items.content:
Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin!

Wiz teams up with Google—what does this mean for cloud security? We take a closer look at compromised GitHub actions and explore ephemeral values in Terraform.

Our featured podcast is about best practices for building a multi-tenant system. We’re also sharing an upcoming event on the future of DevOps and how to build a strong culture to shape better software delivery.

In our tutorials, learn how to handle billions of AWS Lambda invocations, build a powerful March Madness dashboard with Grafana, migrate smoothly from Serverless Framework to AWS CDK, and master data governance using dbt, Terraform, and Dataplex. Scaling Prometheus to the enterprise level, building data ingestion solutions for Amazon Bedrock, and securing Lambda function logs through data masking were also covered.

We’re also spotlighting practical open-source tools, including Stelvio, a Python library that simplifies cloud infrastructure management; Adminer, a simple and ready-to-deploy database management tool; mcp-kafka, which connects AI assistants directly to Apache Kafka; git-who, a handy CLI to quickly discover who wrote specific code; and KHI, a rich log visualization tool that displays Kubernetes logs in an interactive timeline.

All this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin—don’t miss out!

In partnership with Bitrise

DevOps evolution: what's next in 2025?

Get actionable strategies for AI, platform engineering, & DevOps culture to shape software delivery.

  • is a Python library that simplifies cloud infrastructure management and deployment.

  • is a full-featured database management tool written in PHP. It consists of a single file ready to deploy to the target server.

  • provides a bridge between AI assistants and Apache Kafka, allowing them to interact with Kafka clusters through the Model Context Protocol.

  • is a command-line tool for answering that eternal question: Who wrote this code?

  • is a rich log visualization tool for Kubernetes clusters. KHI transforms vast quantities of logs into an interactive, comprehensive timeline view.

If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via , or simply reply to this email.

items.title: Terraform as IaC
items.author: Nikhil Adinath Kamode
items.content:
Here’s a professional and easy-to-understand guide on getting started with Terraform:

Introduction to Terraform and Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

Terraform is a powerful tool for managing infrastructure using Infrastructure as Code (IaC). IaC allows teams to manage and provision infrastructure in a consistent and automated manner, making it an essential component of modern software development. In this guide, we’ll explore the fundamental concepts of Terraform and IaC, and how they streamline infrastructure provisioning.

Key Benefits of Terraform and IaC:

: Ensures that infrastructure configurations are consistent across environments.

: Automates the provisioning process, reducing manual errors.

: Allows tracking of infrastructure changes like code.

Installing Terraform on MacOS, Linux, and Windows

To get started with Terraform, you need to install it on your operating system. Here are the steps for each platform:

: Use Homebrew with the command brew install terraform.

: Download and install from the official Terraform website.

: Download the binary from the official Terraform website and add it to your PATH.

Setting Up Terraform for AWS

To integrate Terraform with AWS, follow these steps:

: Ensure the AWS CLI is installed on your system.

: Run aws configure to set up your AWS credentials.

Set Up AWS Provider in Terraform:

terraform { required_providers { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" version = "~> 5.0" } } } provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }

: Run terraform init to download the AWS provider plugin.

Writing Your First Terraform Code

Start by creating a simple Terraform configuration file (main.tf) to define resources. For example, to create an S3 bucket

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "example_bucket" {  bucket = "example-terraform-bucket"

Understanding the Terraform lifecycle is crucial:

: Initializes the Terraform working directory.

: Generates an execution plan

: Applies the changes.

Launching an EC2 Instance

To provision an EC2 instance on AWS using Terraform

resource "aws_instance" "example_instance" {  ami           = "ami-065deacbcaac64cf2"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  tags = {  }

Terraform state files are essential for managing infrastructure consistency:

: The configuration you want.

: The actual state of your infrastructure.

: Manages these states to ensure consistency.

This guide provides a comprehensive introduction to Terraform and its integration with AWS, helping you get started with managing infrastructure efficiently.

Terraform as IaC was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Install Ansible AWX on Kubernetes (Kind)
items.author: Sandesh Sachdev
items.content:
AWX, the open-source upstream project of Ansible Tower, is a powerful web-based platform for managing Ansible playbooks, inventories, and automation workflows. While AWX is typically deployed on production-grade Kubernetes clusters, this guide provides a detailed walkthrough for setting it up locally using Kind (Kubernetes in Docker). This lightweight approach is perfect for testing, learning, or running small-scale automation projects on a single machine.

In this extensive knowledge base article, we’ll cover every step of the process, from configuring your server to deploying AWX with the AWX Operator, including an external PostgreSQL database and an Ansible playbook for automation. Whether you’re a DevOps enthusiast or an automation newbie, this guide will equip you with the knowledge to get AWX running smoothly.

By the end of this article, you’ll know how to:

  • Install and configure Kind on a Rocky Linux server.
  • Set up an external PostgreSQL database for AWX persistence.
  • Deploy AWX using the AWX Operator on a Kind cluster.

For this setup, I have used a server as the base operating system. Below are the minimum recommended specifications and commands to verify your system:

  1. CPU : 8 Core ( 4 is recommeded )
  2. Memory : 16gb ( 8 is recommeded )

For this Installation, I have created a directory under as a starting point.

Kind is a tool that runs local Kubernetes clusters using Docker containers as nodes. For simplicity and to avoid multi-node complexities, we’ll configure a single-node cluster with a control-plane role.

Create a file at with the following content:

apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4kind: Clusternodes: extraPortMappings: hostPort: 80 protocol: tcp
  • : Defines a single control-plane node for the cluster.
  • : Maps port 80 on the host to port 32000 in the container, enabling HTTP access to AWX via http://<server-ip>:80.

External PostgreSQL Setup

AWX requires a PostgreSQL database for persistent storage. While it includes an embedded database option, using an external PostgreSQL instance offers better control and scalability. Here’s how to set it up:

Step 1: Set Up PostgreSQL

You’ll need an external PostgreSQL server (e.g., on another machine or a cloud service). For this guide, assume it’s reachable at postgres.example.com:5432 with:

    Step 2: Create a Kubernetes Secret

    AWX uses a Kubernetes secret to securely store database credentials. Create a file named awx-postgres-secret.yaml

    apiVersion: v1kind: Secret name: sos-awx-postgres-configurationdata:
     host: cG9zdGdyZXMuZXhhbXBsZS5jb20= # “postgres.example.com” (base64)
     port: NTQzMg== # “5432” (base64)
     database: YXd4 # “awx” (base64)
     username: YXd4dXNlcg== # “awxuser” (base64)
     password: eW91cnBhc3N3b3Jk # “yourpassword” (base64)
     type: dW5tYW5hZ2Vk # “unmanaged” (base64)
     sslmode: ZGlzYWJsZQ== # “disable” (base64)
    type: Opaque

    Convert your values to base64 using:

    $ echo -n “your-value” | base64

    Replace the placeholders with your encoded database details.

    Deploy the secret to your Kind cluster:

    $ kubectl apply -f awx-postgres-secret.yaml

    Docker Storage Configuration

    Docker stores container data in by default, which may fill up quickly on a small root partition. To avoid this, relocate the Docker data directory to

    ( or any other suitable path which has more space ).

    $ sudo mkdir -p /data/docker

    : Edit or create /etc/docker/daemon.json:

    { “data-root”: “/data/docker”
    $ sudo systemctl restart docker`
    $ docker info — format ‘{{.DockerRootDir}}’

    In this setup we are going to uses the following versions:

    • : Rocky Linux 9.x

    Before diving in, ensure these conditions are met:

    1. : No other services should use port that we are going to configure on host ( In this Setup we are going to use port 80 ) . Check with:
    $ sudo netstat -tuln | grep :80
    • Stop conflicting services (e.g., sudo systemctl stop nginx).

    Note : Use single node cluster if Multi Node cluster doesn’t work.

    We will use single node kubernetes cluster to make it simple.

    Step-by-Step Installation

    $ sudo dnf install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io vim git nginx htop nfs-utils$ sudo systemctl enable docker
    $ sudo systemctl start docker
    $ curl -Lo ./kind https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/dl/v0.27.0/kind-linux-amd64$ chmod +x ./kind
    $ sudo mv ./kind /usr/bin/kind
    $ curl -LO “https://dl.k8s.io/release/v1.32.2/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"$ chmod +x kubectl
    $ sudo mv kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl

    Creating Kind Cluster ( Kubernetes Cluster )

    Set up the AWX directory and create the cluster:

    $ sudo mkdir -p /etc/awx# Add kind.config as shown in Kind Configuration
    $ sudo kind create cluster — name awx — config /etc/awx/kind.config — image kindest/node:v1.32.2

    Install Nginx Ingress Controller

    Deploy the Nginx Ingress Controller for Kind:

    $ kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yaml

    Clone the AWX Operator repository

    $ sudo git clone https://github.com/ansible/awx-operator /etc/awx/awx-operator$ cd /etc/awx/awx-operator
    $ sudo git checkout 2.17.0

    Create kustomization.yaml in /etc/awx/awx-operator:

    apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1kind: Kustomization — github.com/ansible/awx-operator/config/default?ref=2.17.0 — name: quay.io/ansible/awx-operatornamespace: awx
    $ kubectl apply -k /etc/awx/awx-operator

    Configure External PostgreSQL

    Apply the secret as described in External PostgreSQL Setup.

    $ kubectl apply -f awx-postgres-secret.yaml

    Create awx-cr.yaml in :

    apiVersion: awx.ansible.com/v1beta1kind: AWX name: sos-awx service_type: nodeport postgres_configuration_secret: sos-awx-postgres-configuration

    Apply the above yaml configuaration

    $ kubectl apply -f /etc/awx/awx-operator/awx-cr.yaml

    You should be able to see the cluster being created.

    $ kubectl get deployments,pods,svc,sc -n awx
    1. If you don’t want to use external postgres server, you can skip installing postgres secret and applying it. As, AWX operator would create a postgres server for you.
    2. Updated awx-cr.yml file would look like
    apiVersion: awx.ansible.com/v1beta1kind: AWX name: sos-awx service_type: nodeport

items.title: Mastering Pointers in C++
items.author: Syed Hamed Raza
items.content:
Pointers are one of the most powerful and essential concepts in C++. They allow , improving efficiency and flexibility. While pointers can be complex, mastering them is crucial for dynamic memory management, data structures (like linked lists), and system-level programming.

This article covers everything about pointers in C++, from basic syntax to advanced concepts like dynamic memory allocation, pointer arithmetic, and smart pointers.

1. Introduction to Pointers

A is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Instead of storing a value directly, it holds a reference to a memory location.

✔ Efficient Memory Management — Enables dynamic memory allocation. ✔  — Direct memory manipulation speeds up computations. ✔ Useful in Data Structures — Essential for linked lists, trees, and graphs. ✔  — Allows modifying variables inside functions efficiently.

Memory Representation of a Pointer

Each variable in memory has:

  • A (actual data stored).
  • An (where the data is stored in RAM).

Syntax of Pointers in C++

type *pointerName;  // Declaring a pointerpointerName = &variable;  // Assigning address of variable

2. Declaring and Initializing Pointers

int num = 10;int *ptr = # // Pointer stores address of num

Example: Declaring and Using Pointers

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int num = 42;
    cout << "Value of num: " << num << endl;
    cout << "Address of num: " << &num << endl;
    cout << "Value using pointer: " << *ptr << endl;
    cout << "Address stored in pointer: " << ptr << endl;}
Value of num: 42Address of num: 0x61ff08Address stored in pointer: 0x61ff08

Pointers support such as:

  • – Moves to the next memory location.
  • – Moves to the previous memory location.
  • – Moves forward by n elements.
  • – Moves backward by n elements.

Example: Pointer Arithmetic

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
    cout << "First element: " << *ptr << endl;
    ptr++;
    cout << "Second element: " << *ptr << endl;
    ptr++;
    cout << "Third element: " << *ptr << endl;
}
First element: 10Second element: 20

Pointers and arrays are closely related. The array name acts like a to its first element.

Example: Accessing Array Elements Using Pointers

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        cout << "Value at arr[" << i << "]: " << *(ptr + i) << endl;}

5. Pointers and Functions

Pointers allow efficient passing of arguments to functions, avoiding unnecessary memory duplication.

Example: Passing Pointers to Functions

#include <iostream>using namespace std;
void increment(int *ptr) {
    (*ptr)++;    int num = 10;
    cout << "Before: " << num << endl;
    increment(&num);
    cout << "After: " << num << endl;
}

6. Dynamic Memory Allocation

Why Dynamic Memory Allocation?

Dynamic memory allocation allows creating variables at runtime using:

  • delete – Deallocates memory.

Example: Using new and delete

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int *ptr = new int(10);
    cout << "Dynamically allocated value: " << *ptr << endl;
    delete ptr;  // Free memory}

Example: Allocating Arrays Dynamically

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int *arr = new int[5];
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        arr[i] = i * 10;
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        cout << "Value at arr[" << i << "]: " << arr[i] << endl;
    delete[] arr;  // Free memory
}

A stores the address of another pointer.

Example: Pointer to Pointer

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    int num = 10;    int **ptr2 = &ptr;
    cout << "Value of num: " << num << endl;
    cout << "Value using pointer: " << *ptr << endl;
    cout << "Value using pointer to pointer: " << **ptr2 << endl;
}

8. Pointers and Structures

Pointers can be used with structures to manipulate .

Example: Using Pointers with Structures

#include <iostream>using namespace std;    string name;};    Student s1 = {"Alice", 20};
    cout << "Student Name: " << ptr->name << endl;
    cout << "Student Age: " << ptr->age << endl;}

9. Smart Pointers in C++ (C++11)

Smart pointers automate memory management, reducing .

  1. – Sole ownership of an object.
  2. – Shared ownership, reference count.
  3. – Non-owning reference.

Example: Using unique_ptr

#include <iostream>#include <memory>    unique_ptr<int> ptr = make_unique<int>(10);
    cout << "Value: " << *ptr << endl;
}

10. Best Practices with Pointers

✔ Always initialize pointers (ptr = nullptr;). ✔ (Always delete allocated memory). ✔ (unique_ptr, shared_ptr). ✔ (ptr = nullptr; after delete).

Pointers with efficient memory management and in C++. From to , mastering pointers enhances program efficiency.

items.title: NATS as a Service Mesh — The Lightweight Superhero Your Microservices Deserve
items.author: Cristhian Ferrufino
items.content:
NATS as a Service Mesh — The Lightweight Superhero Your Microservices Deserve

Welcome back to the ! In our last adventure, we turned you into a containerization wizard with Docker and Kubernetes. Now, it’s time to tackle  — the unsung heroes of microservices communication. Spoiler: NATS is about to make your life way easier.

What’s a Service Mesh, Anyway?

Imagine you’re hosting a party where all your friends (microservices) need to talk to each other. A service mesh is like the ultimate promoter: it handles introductions, ensures no one’s left out, and stops the DJ (your database) from getting overwhelmed. In technical terms, a service mesh manages communication between services, handling load balancing, retries, security, and observability.

But traditional service meshes like and can feel like hiring a promoter who brings their own entourage (sidecars, proxies, and a PhD in YAML). Enter : the lightweight, no-nonsense alternative that’s here to simplify your life.

Service Meshes: The Usual Suspects

Let’s compare the heavyweights:

+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+| Feature            | Istio                         | Linkerd                       | NATS                                     |
+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+
| Complexity         | High (Envoy sidecars, CRDs)   | Moderate (Rust-based proxies) | Low (No sidecars!)                       |
| Latency            | Medium (Proxy overhead)       | Low                           | Ultra-low (Bare metal)                   |
| Setup              | "Read the 50-page manual" 📖  | "Read the 20-page manual" 📘  | "Run `nats-server`" 💻                   |
| Messaging Patterns | Limited (HTTP/gRPC focus)     | Limited (HTTP/gRPC focus)     | Pub/Sub, Request-Reply, Queue Groups 🎯  |
| Learning Curve     | Steeper than a mountain 🏔️    | Hilly ⛰️                      | Gentle slope 🌄                          |
+--------------------+-------------------------------+-------------------------------+------------------------------------------+

Why Traditional Meshes Hurt Your Brain?

  • : Powerful, but you’ll need a dedicated team to manage its sidecars and configurations.
  • : Simpler, but still requires proxies and leans heavily on HTTP/gRPC.
  • : Skips the proxies, embraces simplicity, and handles messaging pattern. 🤯

NATS as a Service Mesh: Less YAML, More Magic ✨

NATS wasn’t *designed* as a service mesh, but its architecture makes it a natural fit. Here’s how it steals the show:

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: nats  replicas: 3    spec:        - name: nats          ports:

NATS uses a binary protocol over TCP, bypassing HTTP overhead. Benchmarks show it handles **1M+ messages/sec** with sub-millisecond latency. Perfect for real-time apps.

3. One Tool, Many Superpowers

  • : Broadcast events to multiple services.
  • : Sync communication, like REST but faster.
  • : Load-balance messages across replicas.
  • : Add persistence when you need it.
nc, _ := nats.Connect("nats://nats:4222")nc.Subscribe("orders.created", func(msg *nats.Msg) {
  fmt.Printf("Order received: %s\n", string(msg.Data))
})
  • : Encrypt traffic between services and NATS with TLS-first Handshake.
  • : with Token Authentication, Plain Text Username/Password, Bcrypted Username/Password, TLS Certificate, NKEY with Challenge or Decentralized JWT Authentication/Authorization to choose from.
  • : With a robust subject-level permissions on a per-user basis.
  • : NATS keeps it straightforward.

When Should You Use NATS as a Service Mesh?

  • You need (IoT, gaming, real-time analytics).
  • You’re tired of managing sidecars and proxies.
  • Your app uses (HTTP, WebSocket or MQTT).
  • You want to without sacrificing power.

Now that your microservices are chatting smoothly with NATS, how do you know if they’re happy? In the next article, we’ll dive into with Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger. Learn how to monitor, trace, and debug your cloud-native apps like a pro. Spoiler: Your future self will thank you when things go sideways at 2 AM. 😅

💬 What’s your #1 struggle with your service mesh? config? Debugging? Share below!

❤️

Happy coding, and stay tuned for the next chapter in the !

items.title: Robots (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
items.author: z0diac
items.content:
Robots (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup

A (small) tribute to I. Asimov.

In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the challenge on TryHackMe. This challenge emphasizes the importance of proper access control and the risks of exposing sensitive directories. By enumerating , we discover hidden directories and retrieve credentials, allowing us to gain an initial foothold. Further privilege escalation via leads to full system compromise. This write-up highlights the dangers of misconfigured access controls, weak credential management, and improperly set file permissions in Linux environments.

To get an overview of the open ports on the target system, perform a ping scan using the following command:

sudo nmap -Pn -vv -T4 -n <ip>

Then to get a full scan, use this following command:

sudo nmap -A -vv -T4 -n <ip>

We notice a robots.txt file, which contains three disallowed entries:

/harming/humans  /ignoring/human/orders

These paths seem to reference Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics (xd), which could be a hint for our next steps. We will investigate these directories further to see if they contain anything useful.

To make navigation easier, we add the target IP to /etc/hosts with the hostname robots.thm

After navigating to the website, we encountered a 403 Forbidden error on the main page. To bypass this restriction, we checked the disallowed paths from robots.txt. Most of them returned the same 403 Forbidden response, except for , which redirected us to a "recruitment campaign" page.

This page includes both a and form, which could be potential attack vectors. We will investigate further.

On the , we notice a hint:

“An admin monitors new users.”

Recalling my write-up, this suggests a possible client-side attack targeting the admin. However, before diving into that, we focus on another crucial detail — .

The page states that the password format follows:

  • username is the chosen username.
  • ddmm represents the of registration.

I proceeded to create an account using:

    Based on the given hashing format, I generated the password using the following command:

    After getting the hash, I headed straight to the login page and logged in.

    Once inside, I noticed a few interesting things. In the top left corner, there’s a link, and below, I can see the admin’s last login timestamp. This could be useful later, so I’ll keep it in mind while exploring the rest of the site.

    Curious about the link, I clicked on it and, to my surprise, it led to a page! This is interesting because exposes a lot of useful information, like server configurations, loaded extensions, and environment variables.

    I took notes on a few interesting things in the section. Specifically, I found:

    • – This might give insights into how session management works.
    • – This shows the internal server IP, which could be useful later.

    Client-Side Attack — Stored XSS

    Let’s jog our memory about the for a second. We noticed two suspicious things earlier:

    1. The message “An admin monitors new users” — suggesting that the admin might manually review new accounts.

      After running the command, I received a response confirming that the file was successfully retrieved.

      Then I attempted to look inside Dolivaw’s home directory by accessing file:///../home/dolivaw/.ssh/, but nothing was returned. However, based on the response body—which did not contain "file/directory not found"—I could confirm that the directory exists.

      With this, we can place our id_rsa.pub key inside Dolivaw's .ssh/authorized_keys file, allowing us to access the account remotely from our machine using our private key.

      But first, we need to generate an SSH key pair using the following command:

      This will create two files:

        We’ll use the public key to gain access to Dolivaw’s account.

        Then I attempted to download your id_rsa.pub file from your web server (http://10.4.61.251:80/id_rsa.pub) and write it to /home/dolivaw/.ssh/authorized_keys.

        After that, I rechecked whether my id_rsa.pub was successfully added by navigating to “file:///home/dolivaw/.ssh/authorized_keys”. Upon checking, I confirmed that my public key was indeed present in the authorized_keys file, meaning I could now authenticate as dolivaw using my private key!

        On my machine, I used SSH to connect to dolivaw@robots.thm using my private key. The authentication was successful, and I gained access to the dolivaw user!

        Root Privilege Escalation

        I ran sudo -l and fount out that the output reveals that the dolivaw user can run /usr/sbin/apache2 as root without requiring a password (NOPASSWD).

        This means there is a potential privilege escalation (privesc) opportunity using Apache2. Since Apache2 is a service that can be manipulated to execute commands as root, we can attempt to exploit this permission to gain root access.

        Based on GTFOBins, we can exploit Apache2’s sudo permission by reading arbitrary files as root using the following command:

        This works because Apache2 runs with elevated privileges when executed via sudo, and the Include directive allows loading arbitrary files.

        Then I tried to follow the GTFOBins method, but it failed. The error message indicated that the APACHE_RUN_DIR variable was not defined.

        Since Apache requires APACHE_RUN_DIR to store its runtime files (such as PID files and logs), I tried setting it manually using:

        export APACHE_RUN_DIR=/tmp

        To manually override this, I tried setting APACHE_RUN_DIR=/tmp and exporting it before running the command. However, this did not work, likely because sudo does not preserve exported environment variables by default when executing commands as another user.

        Instead, I used the following command to explicitly define APACHE_RUN_DIR as /tmp within the execution of Apache itself:

        sudo -u root /usr/sbin/apache2 -C "Define APACHE_RUN_DIR /tmp" -C "Include $LFILE" -k stop
        • sudo -u root /usr/sbin/apache2: Runs the Apache binary as root.
        • -C "Define APACHE_RUN_DIR /tmp": Defines APACHE_RUN_DIR dynamically within Apache’s configuration, bypassing the missing environment variable issue.
        • -C "Include $LFILE": Forces Apache to include an arbitrary file ($LFILE) in its configuration. If $LFILE is a sensitive file (like /root/root.txt), its contents can be exposed.
        • -k stop: Attempts to stop the Apache process, forcing it to parse the injected configuration file before shutting down.

        Since Apache runs with root privileges in this scenario, it successfully reads the target file, allowing me to extract the root flags!

        This challenge demonstrated how exposed files and misconfigurations can lead to a complete system compromise. By enumerating , I was able to uncover hidden directories containing credentials, which provided initial access to the machine. Leveraging these credentials, I escalated privileges by exploiting an SUID binary misconfiguration, ultimately achieving root access.

        This challenge reinforces the importance of restricting sensitive files, enforcing strong access controls, and reviewing file permissions. Any exposed directory or misconfigured binary can serve as a potential attack vector, leading to severe security breaches if not properly mitigated.

I recently encountered issues with my Jenkins builds and needed a fresh start. To keep my CI/CD pipeline clean and efficient, I decided to reset the builds. In this guide, I’ll walk you through the exact steps I took to reset Jenkins builds seamlessly and maintain a smooth development workflow.

If you’re facing similar challenges, follow along as I share my process for resetting Jenkins builds effectively.

Step 1: Logging into Jenkins

The first thing I did was log into my Jenkins server using my credentials.

Step 2: Accessing the Script Console

I navigated to Manage Jenkins from the dashboard.

I scrolled down to the Tools and Actions section and clicked on Script Console.

Step 3: Running the Script

To reset the build history of my Jenkins project, I executed the following Groovy script in the Script Console:

item = Jenkins.instance.getItemByFullName("test-dev")//THIS WILL REMOVE ALL BUILD HISTORY
item.builds.each() { build ->
  build.delete()item.updateNextBuildNumber(1)
item.getNextBuildNumber()

Important: I replaced “test-dev” with the actual name of my Jenkins project before executing the script.

Resetting my Jenkins builds helped me keep my CI/CD pipeline organized and running efficiently.

items.title: Dead Letter Queues: The Key to Keep Your System Reliable
items.author: Muhammad Taufik Akbar
items.content:
Have you ever sent a package that never reached its destination? Maybe the address was wrong, or the recipient refused it. In distributed systems, messages also need a reliable delivery system. But what happens when a message can’t be processed (for whatever reason)? That’s where a comes in.

This article will explain DLQs in simple terms, when to use them (and when not to), different types of DLQs, and the implementation with example in Kotlin

What is a Dead Letter Queue?

A is like a lost-and-found box for messages that fail to be processed. It catches messages that couldn’t be delivered or handled correctly.

Imagine a messaging system like or where producers send messages and consumers process them. If a message is faulty (for example, it has incorrect data that causes an error when being processed), instead of getting lost or causing the system to crash, it moves to a DLQ for later analysis.

  • : The message has missing or invalid data.
  • The consumer application crashes when handling the message.
  • The system tries multiple times but fails.
  • The message wasn’t processed within the allowed time.

When to Use a DLQ (and When Not to)

  • : Instead of discarding failed messages, store them for later debugging.
  • : Understand why certain messages fail and improve the system.
  • : Some errors (e.g., temporary database issues) can be fixed and retried later.
  • Avoid blocking the system: Prevent one bad message from stopping the entire queue.
  • For business logic retries: If a failure is expected (e.g., waiting for an external service), handle retries separately.
  • : Not all failures need to be stored. A high volume of failing messages could overwhelm the DLQ.
  • : DLQs should be a temporary holding place, not a database replacement.

Different messaging systems handle DLQs differently:

1️⃣ RabbitMQ DLQs (Built-in Support). RabbitMQ supports DLQs natively using dead-letter exchanges (DLX), automatically redirecting failed messages to a designated queue.

Example: Implementing a RabbitMQ DLQ in Kotlin (Spring Boot)

@Configurationclass RabbitMQConfig {        const val MAIN_QUEUE = "main.queue"
        const val DLQ = "dead.letter.queue"
    }    fun mainQueue(): Queue {
        return QueueBuilder.durable(MAIN_QUEUE)
            .deadLetterExchange("")
            .deadLetterRoutingKey(DLQ)
            .build()    fun deadLetterQueue(): Queue {
        return QueueBuilder.durable(DLQ).build()
    }

RabbitMQ Consumer Handling with DLQ

@Component@Slf4j
class RabbitMQConsumer(@Autowired private val rabbitTemplate: RabbitTemplate) {
    @RabbitListener(queues = [RabbitMQConfig.MAIN_QUEUE])
    fun consumeMessage(message: String) {            processMessage(message)
            log.info("Processed message: {}", message)
        } catch (e: Exception) {
            log.error("Message failed, sending to DLQ: {}", message, e)
            rabbitTemplate.convertAndSend(RabbitMQConfig.DLQ, message)    }
    private fun processMessage(message: String) {
        if (message.contains("error")) throw Exception("Processing failed")
        log.info("Message has been processed!")
    }

2️⃣ Kafka DLQs (Manual Setup). Unlike RabbitMQ, Kafka does not have a built-in DLQ. Instead, we create a separate topic (e.g., dead-letter-topic) and publish failed messages there for later debugging or retry.

Example: Implementing a Kafka DLQ in Kotlin (Spring Boot)

@Configurationclass KafkaConfig {        const val MAIN_TOPIC = "main-topic"
        const val DLQ_TOPIC = "dead-letter-topic"
    }    fun mainTopic(): NewTopic {
        return TopicBuilder.name(MAIN_TOPIC).partitions(3).replicas(1).build()
    }    fun deadLetterTopic(): NewTopic {
        return TopicBuilder.name(DLQ_TOPIC).partitions(3).replicas(1).build()
    }

Kafka Consumer Handling with DLQ

@Component@Slf4j
class KafkaConsumer(@Autowired private val kafkaTemplate: KafkaTemplate<String, String>) {
    @KafkaListener(topics = [KafkaConfig.MAIN_TOPIC], groupId = "group_id")
    fun consumeMessage(message: String) {            processMessage(message)
            log.info("Processed message: {}", message)
        } catch (e: Exception) {
            log.info("Message failed, sending to DLQ: {}", message)
            kafkaTemplate.send(KafkaConfig.DLQ_TOPIC, message)    }
    private fun processMessage(message: String) {
        if (message.contains("error")) throw Exception("Processing failed")
        log.info("Message has been processed!")
    }

A Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) is an essential mechanism for handling failed messages gracefully. We explored different implementations using RabbitMQ or Kafka in Kotlin (Spring Boot), including handling failed messages and redirecting them to a DLQ.

By implementing a DLQ, we ensure fault tolerance, error analysis, and system reliability. Would you use a DLQ in your system? Let me know your thoughts

items.title: Network Traffic Analysis Using Docker Compose and Wireshark
items.author: Bumi Arya Dirangga
items.content:
Alright, let’s talk about . If you’ve ever wondered how to inspect and understand network traffic in a controlled environment, Docker Compose + Wireshark is the combo you need.

This guide will walk you through setting up a , capturing traffic, and analyzing it like a pro. No unnecessary theory — just stuff you can use today. Let’s go!

Why Use Docker for Traffic Analysis?

You set up a bunch of virtual machines, configure network interfaces, and deal with endless headaches — or you could just use Docker. Why?

✅  — No need to spin up full VMs.✅  — Containers create their own network space.✅  — With , you can define and deploy environments in seconds.✅  — No messy manual configurations. Just run a script, and boom — you’re ready to go.

We’re going to set up a virtual lab environment using Docker Compose to simulate basic network traffic between a web server and a client, then analyze it with Wireshark.

  1. Web Server: Running Nginx to serve HTTP traffic.
  2. Client: An Ubuntu-based container to generate HTTP requests.
  • Wireshark installed on the host machine to capture and analyze network traffic.
  • Basic understanding of Docker, Docker Compose, and networking concepts.
  • Docker and Docker Compose installed on your system.
  • Wireshark installed on your host machine.

i’m using Docker Desktop from windows 11 and enabled wsl integration, so i work it on wsl.

2. Create Project Directory

mkdir network-traffic-labcd network-traffic-lab

3. Define Docker Compose ConfigurationCreate a file named docker-compose.yml with the following content:

version: '3.8'services:    image: nginx:latest
    container_name: web_server
    networks:  client:    container_name: client    networks:    command: bash  my_network:

4. Start the Docker Compose EnvironmentRun the following command to start the containers:

To verify that the containers are running:

5. Prepare the Client ContainerAccess the client container’s shell:

docker exec -it client bash

Inside the container, install curl to simulate HTTP requests:

apt update && apt install -y curl

6. Capture Network Traffic with Wireshark Identify the Docker network interface:

Since I’m using Docker Desktop with WSL, there is no docker0 interface as in regular Linux systems. Docker Desktop uses a network based on Windows NAT or the WSL vEthernet adapter for communication. So, I need to install tcpdump in the client container and export it.

7. Simulate Network Traffic From the client container’s shell, send HTTP requests to the web server:

8. Results and Analysis In Wireshark, filter the captured packets to focus on HTTP traffic using http Examine the packet details:

  • Source/destination IPs (e.g., 172.17.x.x range for Docker’s network).
  • HTTP request/response details (e.g., GET / from client to server).

The captured network traffic in Wireshark includes HTTP request and response packets between the client and web server. The observed traffic confirms successful communication between the containers. Screenshots of captured packets illustrate the packet structure, including source and destination IPs, HTTP headers, and payload data.

This lab provided hands-on exposure to using Docker Compose and Wireshark. By simulating a real-world client-server interaction, we deepened our understanding of packet structures, HTTP transactions, and TCP/IP operations. Such experiments are invaluable for security professionals, network engineers, and system administrators in diagnosing issues and optimizing network performance. Future work could extend this study to include , , and performance tuning strategies in containerized environments.

items.title: Getting Started with LangChain4j: A Beginner’s Guide
items.author: Swapnil Kalbande
items.content:
AI language models are revolutionizing the way we build applications. With LangChain4j, integrating large language models (LLMs) into your Spring Boot application becomes seamless. This guide will help you get started with LangChain4j, set up a Spring Boot project, and build a simple LLM-powered chatbot.

LangChain4j is a Java-based library designed to simplify the integration of LLMs like GPT, LLaMA, and others into your applications. It abstracts the complexities of model interaction, provides conversational memory management, and enables the chaining of multiple tasks for more advanced AI workflows.

Key Features of LangChain4j

  • : Connect with popular models using APIs.
  • : Efficiently handle prompts for natural language conversations.
  • : Maintain conversation context.
  • : Use external APIs for dynamic results.

Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:

  • Basic knowledge of Spring Boot

Step 1: Setting Up Your Spring Boot Application

1. Create a Spring Boot Project

    2. Add LangChain4j Dependency

    Add the following to your pom.xml file:

    <dependency>  <groupId>dev.langchain4j</groupId>
      <artifactId>langchain4j-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
      <version>1.0.0</version>

    Step 2: Configure LangChain4j

    In your application.properties or application.yml, configure your API key (e.g., using OpenAI GPT):

    langchain4j.api-key=your-openai-api-keylangchain4j.model=gpt-4

    Step 3: Build a Simple Chatbot

    Create a chatbot service using LangChain4j’s simple interface.

    import dev.langchain4j.model.chat.ChatLanguageModel;import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;public class ChatService {
        private final ChatLanguageModel chatLanguageModel;
        public ChatService(ChatLanguageModel chatLanguageModel) {
            this.chatLanguageModel = chatLanguageModel;
        }
        public String chat(String userMessage) {
            return chatLanguageModel.generate(userMessage);}
    import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.*;@RequestMapping("/chat")
    public class ChatController {
        private final ChatService chatService;
        public ChatController(ChatService chatService) {
            this.chatService = chatService;    @PostMapping
        public String chat(@RequestBody String message) {
            return chatService.chat(message);}

    Step 4: Test Your Chatbot

    Start your Spring Boot application using:

    Send a POST request using curl or Postman:

    curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/chat \-H "Content-Type: application/json" \

    Congratulations! You’ve built a simple chatbot using LangChain4j. From here, you can enhance your chatbot by adding conversational memory, integrating external APIs, or chaining multiple tasks. Stay tuned for more advanced tutorials!

items.title: Leveraging Docker with TensorFlow Models & TensorFlow.js for a Snake AI Game
items.author: Harsh Manvar
items.content:
The emergence of containerization has brought about a significant transformation in software development and deployment by providing a consistent and scalable environment on many platforms. For developers trying to optimize their operations, Docker in particular has emerged as the preferred technology.  By using containers as the foundation, developers gain the same key benefits — portability, scalability, efficiency, and security — that they rely on for other workloads, seamlessly extending them to ML and AI applications. Using a real-world example involving a Snake AI game, we’ll examine in this article how TensorFlow.js can be used with Docker to run AI/ML in a web browser.

Why Docker for TensorFlow.js conversion?

With the help of TensorFlow.js, a robust toolkit, machine learning models can be executed in a web browser, opening up a plethora of possibilities for applications such as interactive demonstrations and real-time inference. Docker offers a sophisticated solution by guaranteeing consistency and user-friendliness throughout the conversion process by enclosing it inside a container.

The Snake AI neural network game

The brings a modern twist to the classic Snake game by integrating artificial intelligence that learns and improves its gameplay over time. In this version, you can either play manually using arrow keys or let the AI take control. 

The AI running with continuously improves by making strategic movements to maximize the score while avoiding collisions. The game runs in a browser using , allowing you to test different trained models and observe how the AI adapts to various challenges. 

Whether you’re playing for fun or experimenting with AI models, this game is a great way to explore the intersection of gaming and machine learning. In our approach, we’ve used the neural network to play the traditional Snake game.

Before we dive into the AI in this Snake game, let’s understand the basics of a neural network.

What is a neural network?

A is a type of machine learning model inspired by the way the human brain works. It’s made up of layers of nodes (or “neurons”), where each node takes some inputs, processes them, and passes an output to the next layer.

Key components of a neural network:

  • Receives the raw data (in our game, the snake’s surroundings).
  • Process the input and extract patterns.
  • Gives the final prediction

Figure 1: Key components of a

Imagine each neuron as a small decision-maker. The more neurons and layers, the better the network can recognize patterns.

There are several types of neural networks, each suited for different tasks!

  1. Feedforward Neural Networks (FNNs):
  • The simplest type, where data flows in one direction, from input to output.
  • Great for tasks like classification, regression, and pattern recognition.
  1. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs):
  • Designed to work with image data.
  • Uses filters to detect spatial patterns, like edges or textures.
  1. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
  • Good for sequence prediction (e.g., stock prices, text generation).
  • Remembers previous inputs, allowing it to handle time-series data.
  1. Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTMs)
  • A specialized type of RNN that can learn long-term dependencies.
  1. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
  • Used for generating new data, such as creating images or deepfakes.
  • Image classification, object detection, facial recognition.
  • Language models, stock market prediction, time-series data.
  • Games like Snake, where the task is to predict the next action based on the current state.

The snake game offers two ways to play:

  • You control the snake using your keyboard’s arrow keys.
  • The goal is to eat the fruit (red square) without hitting the walls or yourself.
  • Every time you eat a fruit, the snake grows longer, and your score increases.
  • If the snake crashes, the game ends, and you can restart to try again.
  • The game plays itself using a neural network (a type of AI brain) built with TensorFlow.js.
  • The AI looks at the snake’s surroundings (walls, fruit location, and snake body) and predicts the best move: left, forward, or right.
  • After each game, the AI learns from its mistakes and becomes smarter over time.
  • With enough training, the AI can avoid crashing and improve its score.

Figure 2: The classic Nokia snake game with an AI twist

Let’s go through how this game is built step by step. You’ll first need to install Docker to run the game in a web browser. Here’s a summary of the steps. 

  • Access the game using the web browser

git clone https://github.com/dockersamples/snake-game-tensorflow-docker

  • A supported version of Mac, Linux, or Windows

Click here to download Docker Desktop. Select the version appropriate for your system (Apple Silicon or Intel chip for Mac users, Windows, or Linux distribution)

Figure 3: Download Docker Desktop from the Docker website for your machine

After installing Docker Desktop, run the pre-built Docker image andexecute the following command in your command prompt. It’ll pull the image and start a new container running the Docker image and expose port on the host machine. 

Run the following command to bring up the application:

Next, open the browser and go to http://localhost:8080 to see the output of the snake game and start your first game.

Why use Docker to run the snake game?

  • No need to install Nginx on your machine — Docker handles it.
  • The game runs the same way on any system that supports Docker.
  • You can easily share your game as a Docker image, and others can run it with a single command.

The acts as the foundation of the game, defining the layout and structure of the webpage. It fetches the library , which powers the AI, along with for handling gameplay logic and for AI-based movements. The game UI is simple yet functional, featuring a that lets players switch between manual control (using arrow keys) and AI mode. The dynamically updates the score, high score, and generation count when the AI is training. Also, the game itself runs on an HTML element, making it highly interactive. As we move forward, we’ll explore how the JavaScript files bring this game to life!

The HTML file sets up the structure of the game, like the game canvas and control buttons. It also fetches the library from Tensorflow which will be further used by the code to train the snake. 

  • Where the game is drawn.
  • Lets you switch between manual and AI gameplay.
  • The library that powers the AI brain!

This file handles everything in the game—drawing the board, moving the snake, placing the fruit, and keeping score.

const canvas = document.getElementById('gameCanvas');
const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');

let snake = [{ x: 5, y: 5 }];
let fruit = { x: 10, y: 10 };
let direction = { x: 1, y: 0 };
let score = 0;
  • Where the snake starts.
  • Where the apple is.
  • Which way the snake is moving.

The game loop keeps the game running, updating the snake’s position, checking for collisions, and handling the score.

function gameLoopManual() {
    const head = { x: snake[0].x + direction.x, y: snake[0].y + direction.y };

    if (head.x === fruit.x && head.y === fruit.y) {
        score++;
        fruit = placeFruit();
    } else {
        snake.pop();
    }
    snake.unshift(head);
}
  • Adds a new head to the snake and removes the tail (unless it eats an apple).
  • If the head hits the wall or its own body, the game ends.
document.getElementById('mode').addEventListener('change', function() {
    gameMode = this.value;
});
  • Use arrow keys to control the snake.
  • The neural network predicts the next move.
function gameOver() {
    clearInterval(gameInterval);
    alert('Game Over');
}

function resetGame() {
    score = 0;
    snake = [{ x: 5, y: 5 }];
    fruit = placeFruit();
}
  • Stops the game when the snake crashes.
  • Resets the game back to the beginning.

This file creates and trains the neural network — the AI brain that learns how to play Snake!

var movementOptions = ['left', 'forward', 'right'];

const neuralNet = tf.sequential();
neuralNet.add(tf.layers.dense({units: 256, inputShape: [5]}));
neuralNet.add(tf.layers.dense({units: 512}));
neuralNet.add(tf.layers.dense({units: 256}));
neuralNet.add(tf.layers.dense({units: 3}));
  • This is a simulated brain with four layers of neurons.
  • Information about the game state (walls, fruit position, snake body).
  • One of three choices: turn left, go forward, or turn right.
const optAdam = tf.train.adam(.001);
neuralNet.compile({
  optimizer: optAdam,
  loss: 'meanSquaredError'
});
  • Helps the brain learn efficiently by adjusting its weights.
  • Measures how wrong the AI’s predictions are, helping it improve.

Every time the snake plays a game, it remembers its moves and trains itself.

async function trainNeuralNet(moveRecord) {
  for (var i = 0; i < moveRecord.length; i++) {
     const expected = tf.oneHot(tf.tensor1d([deriveExpectedMove(moveRecord[i])], 'int32'), 3).cast('float32');
     posArr = tf.tensor2d([moveRecord[i]]);
     await neuralNet.fit(posArr, expected, { batchSize: 3, epochs: 1 });
     expected.dispose();
     posArr.dispose();
  }
}

After each game, the AI looks at what happened, adjusts its internal connections, and tries to improve for the next game.

The movementOptions array defines the possible movement directions for the snake: ‘left’, ‘forward’, and ‘right’.

An Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001 compiles the model, and a mean squared error loss function is specified. The trainNeuralNet function is defined to train the neural network using a given moveRecord array. It iterates over the moveRecord array, creates one-hot encoded tensors for the expected movement, and trains the model using the TensorFlow.js fit method.

When playing, the AI predicts what the best move should be.

function computePrediction(input) {
  let inputs = tf.tensor2d([input]);
  const outputs = neuralNet.predict(inputs);
  return movementOptions[outputs.argMax(1).dataSync()[0]];
}

The function makes predictions using the trained neural network. It takes an input array, creates a tensor from the input, predicts the movement using the neural network, and returns the movement option based on the predicted output.

The code demonstrates the creation of a neural network model, training it with a given move record, and making predictions using the trained model. This approach can enhance the snake AI game’s performance and intelligence by learning from its history and making informed decisions.

FROM nginx:latest
COPY . /usr/share/nginx/html
  • This tells Docker to use the latest version of as the base image.
  • is a web server that’s great for serving static files like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript ( (which is what the Snake game is made of).
  • Instead of creating a server from scratch, the following line saves time by using an existing, reliable Nginx setup.

COPY . /usr/share/nginx/html

  • This line copies everything from your current project directory (the one with the Snake game files: index.html, script.js, ai.js, etc.) into the Nginx web server’s default folder for serving web content.
  • /usr/share/nginx/html is the default folder where Nginx looks for web files to display.

Here’s how you can set up the development environment for the Snake game using Docker!

To make the development smoother, you can use s to avoid rebuilding the Docker image every time you change the game files. 

Run the command from the folder where Snake-AI-TensorFlow-Docker code exists:

docker run -it --rm -d -p 8080:80 --name web -v ./:/usr/share/nginx/html nginx

docker: Error response from daemon: Mounts denied: 
The path /Users/harsh/Downloads/Snake-AI-TensorFlow-Docker is not shared from the host and is not known to Docker.
You can configure shared paths from Docker -> Preferences... -> Resources -> File Sharing.
See https://docs.docker.com/desktop/settings/mac/#file-sharing for more info.

Open Docker Desktop, go to Settings -> Resources -> File Sharing -> Select the location where you cloned the repository code, and click on .

Figure 4: Using Docker Desktop to set up a development environment for the AI snake game

Run the command again, you won’t face any errors now 

docker run -it --rm -d -p 8080:80 --name web -v ./:/usr/share/nginx/html nginx

Check if the container is running with the following command

harsh@Harshs-MacBook-Air snake-game-tensorflow-docker % docker ps
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE     COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                  NAMES
c47e2711b2db   nginx     "/docker-entrypoint.…"   3 seconds ago   Up 2 seconds   0.0.0.0:8080->80/tcp   web

Open the browser and go to the URL http://localhost:8080, you’ll see the snake game output. This setup is perfect for development because it keeps everything fast and dynamic. 

Figure 5: Accessing the snake game via a web browser.

Changes in the code will be reflected right away in the browser without rebuilding the container-v ~/.:/usr/share/nginx/html — This is the magic part! It mounts your local directory (Snake-AI-TensorFlow-Docker) into the Nginx HTML directory () inside the container.

Any changes you make to HTML, CSS, or JavaScript files in code ~/Snake-AI-TensorFlow-Docker immediately reflect in the running app without needing to rebuild the container.

In conclusion, building a Snake AI game using TensorFlow.js and Docker demonstrates how seamlessly ML and AI can be integrated into interactive web applications. Through this project, we’ve not only explored the fundamentals of reinforcement learning but also seen firsthand how Docker can simplify the development and deployment process.

By containerizing, Docker ensures a consistent environment across different systems, eliminating the common “it works on my machine” problem. This consistency makes it easier to collaborate with others, deploy to production, and manage dependencies without worrying about version mismatches or local configuration issues for web applications, machine learning projects, or AI applications.

items.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: AWS Pi Day, Amazon Bedrock multi-agent collaboration, Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio, Amazon S3 Tables, and more
items.author: Prasad Rao
items.content:
This year’s virtual event featured in-depth discussions with Amazon Web Services (AWS) product teams showcasing our continued innovation in helping customers build robust data foundations for analytics and AI workloads.

Missed the live event? You can still access all content on-demand at the event page. Whether you’re developing data lakehouses, training AI models, creating generative AI applications, or optimizing analytics workloads, the shared insights will help you maximize the value of your data.

Here are some launches that got my attention during the previous week.

Amazon Bedrock now supports multi-agent collaboration – With the availability of multi-agent collaboration in Amazon Bedrock, you can create networks of specialized agents that communicate and coordinate under the guidance of a supervisor agent. You can build, deploy, and manage networks of AI agents that work together to execute complex, multi-step workflows efficiently.

Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio is now generally available – You can now use Amazon SageMaker Unified Studio as your single data and AI development environment, where you can find and access all of your organization’s data and work using the best tools for your specific needs. With the new simplified permissions management, you can easily bring your existing AWS resources into the unified studio. You’ll be able to find, access, and query your organization’s data and AI assets while collaborating with your team to securely build and share your analytics and AI artifacts—from data and models to generative AI applications.

Amazon S3 reduces pricing for S3 object tagging by 35% – Amazon S3 reduces pricing for S3 object tagging by 35% in all AWS Regions to $0.0065 per 10,000 tags per month. Object tags are key-value pairs applied to S3 objects that can be created, updated, or deleted at any time during the lifetime of the object.

Here are some of my favorite posts from community.aws. Create your AWS Builder ID to start sharing your tips and connect with fellow builders. Your Builder ID is a universal login credential that gives you access, beyond the AWS Management Console, to AWS tools and resources, including over 600 free training courses, community features, and developer tools such as Amazon Q Developer.

Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events:

AWS Summits – The AWS Summit season is coming along! Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Paris (April 9), Amsterdam (April 16), London (April 30), and Poland (May 5).

AWS re:Inforce (June 16–18) – Our annual learning event devoted to all things AWS Cloud security in Philadelphia, PA. Registration opens in March, so be ready to join more than 5,000 security builders and leaders.

AWS DevDays are free, technical events where developers can learn about some of the hottest topics in cloud computing. DevDays offer hands-on workshops, technical sessions, live demos, and networking with AWS technical experts and your peers. Register to access AWS DevDays sessions on demand.

That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!

This post is part of our Weekly Roundup series. Check back each week for a quick roundup of interesting news and announcements from AWS!

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

items.title: Configuring Jenkins SSH-Key Agent to Connect to Remote Servers
items.author: Eilony Pearl
items.content:
I recently needed to set up Jenkins to securely connect to remote servers for automated deployments. Instead of using password-based authentication, I decided to configure SSH key-based authentication for better security and efficiency. In this guide, I’ll walk you through the exact steps I followed to get it working seamlessly.

Step 1: Generating SSH Keys on the Remote Server

First, I logged in to my remote server and generated SSH keys to establish secure authentication.

Step 2: Verifying SSH Key Generation

To confirm that the SSH keys were successfully created, I ran:

Here’s what the output looked like:

root@server1:~# ls -lh .ssh/total 8.0K
-rw - - - - 1 root root 0 Apr 15 06:14 authorized_keys
-rw - - - - 1 root root 2.6K Apr 30 01:47 id_rsa
-rw-r - r - 1 root root 578 Apr 30 01:47 id_rsa.pub

Step 3: Adding the Public Key to the Authorized Keys File

To allow Jenkins to authenticate without a password, I added my public key to the authorized_keys file:

cat .ssh/id_rsa.pub >> .ssh/authorized_keys

Step 4: Copying the Private Key

Since Jenkins requires the private key to authenticate, I viewed and copied it using:

I made sure to store it securely.

Step 5: Navigating to Jenkins Credentials Manager

Next, I logged into Jenkins and followed these steps:

Scrolled down to Security and clicked Credentials.

Under Stores Scoped to Jenkins, I clicked System.

Then, under System, I selected Global credentials (unrestricted).

Clicked Add Credentials to create a new entry.

Step 6: Adding SSH Credentials to Jenkins

I filled in the required fields as follows:

  • : SSH Username with Private Key
  • : Global (Jenkins, nodes, items, all child items, etc.)
  • : (Chose a unique name for reference)
  • : (Used the same as ID for clarity)
  • : root (or the appropriate remote server username)
  • : Pasted the private key from Step 4

After clicking Create, Jenkins successfully stored my SSH credentials.

With this setup, I can now securely connect Jenkins to my remote servers for automation without worrying about passwords. It makes deployments smoother and more secure.

items.title: Understanding Principal Component Analysis And Creating it (kinda) With Python
items.author: ishan
items.content:
Principal Component Analysis, or , is a technique used in data analysis to reduce the complexity of data while preserving as much of the important information as possible. It’s often used in machine learning, statistics, and data science to simplify large datasets, find patterns, and make the data easier to analyze and visualize.

In this blog, I’ll explain in simple terms, and walk through the two common methods used to calculate PCA:

  1. By creating the covariance matrix and then using eigen decomposition.
  2. By using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to get the components.

Imagine you have a large dataset with many features (variables) that describe each data point. For example, let’s say you have a dataset about houses, and the features include the number of rooms, the area, the price, and the location of each house.

If you plot all of these features in a graph, you might find that the data is spread out in a complex way. But even though it looks complicated, there’s often a simple underlying structure to the data — for example, house price might depend mostly on area, and the number of rooms might not change much independently of the area.

PCA helps simplify the data by identifying the key directions (called ) in which the data varies the most. Instead of working with all the original features, we can project the data onto just a few key directions, reducing the number of features and making the dataset easier to analyze.

  • in the data by identifying the of maximum variation (i.e., the directions along which the data is most spread out).
  • It then by keeping only the most important patterns and discarding the rest.
  • : It simplifies the data by reducing the number of features, which can make machine learning models faster and easier to interpret.
  • : It helps us reduce high-dimensional data (with many features) into two or three dimensions, so we can visualize it more easily.
  • : It helps filter out noisy or irrelevant features in the data.
  • : It allows us to find the most important combinations of features in the data.

Two Methods to Calculate PCA

Now, let’s look at the two common methods to calculate PCA: Covariance Matrix + Eigen Decomposition and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).

Method 1: Using Covariance Matrix and Eigen Decomposition

This method involves a few key steps:

  • First, we standardize the data so that each feature has a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. This is important because PCA is sensitive to the scale of the features (i.e., the range of values). Without this step, features with larger ranges would dominate the analysis.

2. Calculate the Covariance Matrix:

  • The covariance matrix tells us how the features in the data relate to one another. It shows whether two features change in the same direction (positive covariance) or in opposite directions (negative covariance).
  • For a dataset with n features, the covariance matrix will be an n×n matrix that captures the relationships between each pair of features.
  • The next step is to perform an on the covariance matrix. This involves finding the and of the covariance matrix.
  • tell us how much variance (spread) is captured by each principal component.
  • (or eigenvectors corresponding to the largest eigenvalues) tell us the directions (principal components) along which the data has the most variation.

4. Sort the Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors:

  • Once you have the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, you sort them in descending order, so the principal components that capture the most variance come first.
  • Finally, you project the data onto the new directions (the eigenvectors), reducing the dimensionality of the dataset while preserving as much variance as possible.
  • We create a to understand how the features relate to each other,
  • Then, we find the (directions) and (importance of those directions) to identify the principal components.

Method 2: Using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

The second method to calculate PCA uses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). SVD is a technique from linear algebra that decomposes any matrix into three matrices: , , and . This method is more computationally efficient and is often used in practice.

Here’s how SVD helps us calculate PCA:

  • We apply SVD to the data matrix A, which has the dimensions m×n, (where m is the number of data points and n is the number of features).
  • The SVD of A gives us three matrices:
  • : Contains the left singular vectors, representing the new coordinates of the data points after transformation.
  • : A diagonal matrix containing the singular values (which are the square roots of the eigenvalues), indicating how much variance is explained by each principal component.
  • : Contains the right singular vectors, which correspond to the principal components (the directions of maximum variance).

2. Extract Principal Components:

  • From , we extract the (the directions of maximum variance).
  • The matrix tells us how much variance each of these principal components explains.
  • Finally, to reduce the data’s dimensionality, we project the data onto the selected principal components by multiplying A with the transpose of the principal components matrix.
  • directly gives us the (from V^T) and how much variance each component explains (from Σ).
  • It’s a more computationally efficient way to calculate PCA compared to using the covariance matrix and eigen decomposition.

Key Differences Between the Two Methods

  1. Covariance Matrix + Eigen Decomposition:
  • Works by first computing the covariance matrix, which requires calculating pairwise relationships between features.
  • It’s more conceptually intuitive, but might be less efficient for large datasets because you need to compute and store the covariance matrix, which can be large.
  • SVD is more efficient for large datasets because it works directly on the data matrix without needing to explicitly calculate the covariance matrix.
  • It’s computationally more stable and is the method used by most PCA implementations in practice.

Here is the code implementation of PCA using SVD:

Although it is not in OOP format, it is easy to understand for any beginner.

import numpy as np
# Sample data matrix (e.g., 4 data points and 3 features)
A = np.array([[1, 2, 3],              [7, 8, 9],
# Step 1: Apply numpy SVD
U, Sigma, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A, full_matrices=False)
# Step 2: Calculate the squared singular values (variance explained)
singular_values_squared = Sigma**2
total_variance = np.sum(singular_values_squared)
variance_explained_percentage = (singular_values_squared / total_variance) * 100
# Step 3: Calculate the cumulative variance
cumulative_variance = np.cumsum(variance_explained_percentage)
# Step 4: Keep enough components to explain 90% of the variance
threshold = 90  
num_components = np.argmax(cumulative_variance >= threshold)
# Step 5: Project data onto the first `num_components` principal components
V_k = Vt[:num_components + 1, :] 
# V is selected as it is the input space matrix, and we transpose it to match A
print("Number of components chosen:", num_components)
print("Cumulative variance explained:", cumulative_variance)
print("Reduced data representation:\n", A_pca)

I hope this helps you in learning and understanding PCA, happy coding :)
